generalities.text: Main THE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF PURE MATERIA MEDICA By TIMOTHY F. ALLEN, A.M., M.D. Presented by Médi-T CORIARIA RUSCIFOLIA. C. ruscifolia, Linn. Nat. order , Coriarieæ (between Anacardiaceæ and Rutaceæ). Common names , Toot-berry, Tupa-kihi or Tutu (New Zealand species). Preparation , Tincture or trituration of the berries. Authorities. 1 , W. Lander Lindsay, M.D., F. R. S., Br. and For. Med.-Chir. Rev., 1868, 2, 163, general effects of eating the berries; 2 , Mr. Martin ate 1/2 pint of the berries, ibid.; 3 , a young man ate old beries, with the seeds, ibid.; 4 , a man ate a large quantity of the berries, including the seeds, ibid.; 5 , a girl nearly lost her life by eating the berries, ibid.; 6 , effect of swallowing the berry with the small stalk adhering to it, ibid.; 7 , M. H. Rev., 9, 278, general effects; 8 , J. Giles, M. H. Rev., 10, 187, effects on a man. MIND. ► Emotional. ► Sometimes there are symptoms like those of brain fever, [1] . ► The symptoms differed greatly in different individuals. Some became excited or violent; they laughed or they raved outrageously, or their conduct was simply grotesque in the extreme; the results, indeed, closely resembled those producible by "laughing gas." Others appeared as if under the influence of alcoholic stimulants, [1] . ► Occasionally the delirium resembles that of alcoholic intoxication, or delirium tremens; at other times it rather approximates that of acute mania, being marked by great muscular excitement, the patient requiring restraint of the most powerful kind, in some instances the assistance of several strong men, for several hours, [1] . ► It is called "Tupa-kihi" by the New Zealanders, because persons under its influence resemble in their actions "drunken Europeans," [7] . ► During the night he was seized with delirium , accompanied by great muscular excitement, resembling that of acute mania, necessitating the assistance of several men to restrain him, [4] . ► Felt as if he had been newly born into the world, and was overwhelmed with wonder at the novelty of all his surroundings (third day), [2] . ► Intellectual. ► One of the characteristics of the convalescent stage is loss of memory, with or without a vertiginous condition, [1] . ► On emerging from the comatose condition, loss of memory was the chief or only peculiarity observable. For about half a day he remained in a semi-stupid state, not able to remember where he was or what he had been doing for the previous forty-eight hours, or how he had come there (third day), [2] . ► When convalescing from the stupor, resembled a person emerging from a brain fever. Was utterly oblivious of the particulars of his illness, denying even that he had eaten the berries, [3] . [10.] ► The next day he was pretty well, but had entirely lost his memory of all recent occurrences. He could not tell me even to what detachment of the regiment he belonged, or at what post he had been stationed. This loss of memory seems to be a characteristic effect of this plant, [8] . ► Total loss of consciousness, [8] . ► When he attempted to get out of bed, at about 6 A.M., he got on his clothes partly, but suddenly lost all consciousness till about 11 A.M. (second day). He was conscious for a minute or two, but almost immediately relapsed into stupor, which continued for about twenty-four hours, [2] . ► Stupor, [1] . ► State of stupor continuing all the next day, following the convulsions, [3] . ► Coma, with or without convulsions or delirium, [1] . HEAD. ► Giddiness, [1] . ► It seems to act principally on the intellectual or parietal portion of the brain, producing great excitement, followed by congestion. Its action is rapid, [7] . STOMACH. ► Retching, [5] . ► Vomiting, [5] . GENERALITIES. [20.] ► Convulsions, [5] . ► Seized with convulsions, which speedily became so violent that it required two strong men to hold him. These convulsions were severe for about forty minutes, when they gradually diminished, and the patient passed into a state of stupor (after five hours), [3] . ► Tetanic convulsions. From his appearance I should have certainly supposed that he had taken strychnia. The convulsions were very violent and the opisthotonos complete, [8] . ► Attack similar to epilepsy (from swallowing berry with small stalk adhering), [6] . ► A peculiar form of nervous irritability not observable prior to the Toot poisoning (after several years). It is questionable, however, whether this sequela is a " propter hoc," or a mere " post hoc," [5] . SUPPLEMENT: CORIARIA RUSCIFOLIA. Authority. 9 , H. G. Hughes, Pharm. Journ., Third Ser., vol. ii, 1871, p. 281, ate about 1 1/2 grains of the resin. ► Disagreeable irritating sensation in the throat, extending to the stomach, with pain across the region of the stomach, accompanied by nausea (in five minutes). In a quarter of an hour vomiting came on, which continued more or less for two hours. Very unpleasant sensations continued for two hours more, when, after great flushing of the face, with all but intolerable heat, the effects passed away, [9] . Copyright © Médi-T ® 2000 Main
Clinical: Delirium tremens; Mania; Memory lost; Vomiting

keynotes.text: Coriaria produces intoxication very like that of alcohol. Great muscular activity as in acute mania, requiring several persons to hold a person when under the influence of the drug. The most marked feature is complete loss of memory, which ensues after coming out of the acute stage of the poisoning. A prover who ate 1 1/2 gr. of the resin experienced the following: Disagreeable, irritating sensation in throat, extending to stomach, with pain across region of stomach, accompanied by nausea (in five min). In a quarter of an hour vomiting came on, continuing more or less for two hours. Very unpleasant sensations continued for two hours more, when, after great flushing of the face, with all but intolerable heat, the effects passed away. Copyright © Médi-T ® 2000 Main
